thread.title: UVA partners with ODNI to launch National Security Data and Policy Institute | News | cbs19news.com
thread.title_full: UVA partners with ODNI to launch National Security Data and Policy Institute | News | cbs19news.com

author: Mark Gad
title: UVA partners with ODNI to launch National Security Data and Policy Institute | News | cbs19news.com
text:
CHARLOTTESVILLE, Va. (CBS19 NEWS) -- A ribbon-cutting for the University of Virginia's new National Security Data and Policy Institute was held on Friday.
In October, UVA secured a $20M contract with the Office of the Director of National Intelligence to launch the institute, designed to combat threats like Russia, Iran, China and North Korea.
"Traditionally, the spying business is spying on another government or spying on another military. Most of national security today is really technology competition," said Virginia Senator Mark Warner.
Warner emphasized that none of these nations present a greater threat than China.
"The Chinese have infiltrated all of our telecom networks, which means they can pick individuals, listen to their calls, and read their text messages."
The institute will combine UVA's strengths in data science, artificial intelligence, research computing and public policy.
"These types of efforts are absolutely critical to ensuring that we are setting up policymakers in technology, cybersecurity, and a whole range of areas where, unless we undertake these types of efforts, we're not going to be able to keep up with our strategic competition," said Charles Luftig, deputy director of National Intelligence for Policy and Capabilities.
Philip Potter, the institute's executive director, believes this initiative can bridge two important worlds — government and data.
"Our policy folks are often really good at identifying the right questions. Our data scientists are very good at knowing how to predict the right patterns. Bringing those two things together presents really exciting opportunities," Potter said.
Warner also emphasized that from Day One, he advocated for the institute to be based in Virginia, saying he can't think of a better place than UVA to lead this critical effort.

organizations.name: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
